title: Becoming an Angel: The Mundus Imaginalis and the Platonic Path of Self-Knowledge
author: Angela Voss

body.text: Angela Voss opens with an unexpected angle: alchemy. Not the laboratory kind, but the inner work of transmuting the soul. For Henry Corbin, alchemy was never about making gold. It was about the juncture between spirit and matter, the hidden secret that symbols point to. This secret is accessed through the mundus imaginalis, the intermediate world where the spiritual becomes perceptible and the material becomes transparent.

Alchemy for Corbin is essentially the inner, spiritual work of attaining union between the human soul and its heavenly counterpart within the mysterious ground of the 'hidden secret' pointed to by the symbolic image. This is the intermediate place between spirit and matter, the mundus imaginalis, where the spiritual world assumes an objective reality, and where the transmutation of the prima materia of the human psyche into the subtle or spiritual body is the work of an alchemical opus that involves encounter with an angelic presence through the faculty of the active imagination. In this paper, I intend to explore the nature of this encounter in the context of the neoplatonism of the Islamic mystical philosophers to whom Corbin dedicated his life and work.

Before diving into Corbin's cosmology, Voss establishes a crucial distinction between two modes of knowing. One is Aristotelian: rational, analytical, the observer separate from the observed. The other is Platonic: participatory, intuitive, the knower united with the known. Corbin's entire project sits firmly in the second camp.

To begin, we should briefly consider the kind of knowledge that characterises the Platonic path of gnosis. One of the ways the Aristotelian and Platonic methods of philosophising were distinguished in the Renaissance was through the assignment of a 'human' mode of investigation to the former, and a 'divine' mode of revelation to the latter. Human modes were characterised by rational, theoretical and analytical attempts to grasp the world of nature through observation and deduction, whereas divine modes embodied a deep intuitive sense of transcendent principles governing and emanating throughout creation, apprehended only through the highest intellectual principle in the soul which recognised the images of its divine source. The former entailed the separation of the observer from the object observed, the latter direct participation in it in order to know it. The former took place in time, the latter in a timeless place beyond the working out of cause and effect. Such contrasting modes lie behind the statement of Henry Corbin that 'the Active Imagination is not a theory, it is an initiation to vision.'

Voss traces this Platonic epistemology through Iamblichus, who distinguished between human reasoning about the gods and the soul's direct participation in divine reality through theurgic practice. Corbin's scholarship was not academic in the modern sense; it was an initiation into the tradition he studied.
Voss gives a brief biography of Corbin to show how he arrived at his syncretic vision. It matters because his work was not detached scholarship but a lived path.

Corbin was born in Paris in 1903 and came into contact with Louis Massignon, Director of Islamic studies at the Sorbonne. When Massignon gave him a volume of the 12th-century Persian mystic Suhrawardi, Corbin wrote: 'through my meeting with Suhrawardi, my spiritual destiny for the passage through this world was sealed. Platonism, expressed in terms of the Zoroastrian angelology of ancient Persia, illuminated the path that I was seeking.' He was also studying the Protestant theologians and the hermeneutics of Martin Heidegger. In 1939 he went to Istanbul and Teheran for seven years to immerse himself in Islamic mysticism. He attended the Eranos conferences along with Carl Jung, Mircea Eliade and Gershom Scholem. It was in the prophetic mysticism of the Sufi tradition that Corbin found the central inspiration for his life work: the integration of Hermetic and neoplatonic gnosis into a spiritual path of the imagination.

The cosmology that underlies everything is a three-tiered universe: the divine intelligible realm, the material realm, and between them a mediating dimension that partakes of both. This middle realm is the mundus imaginalis. It is not a static layer but a dynamic field of personified forces.

Neoplatonic cosmology consists of three planes of reality: the divine intelligible realm, the material realm, and linking them a mediating dimension which partakes of both, a spiritual body or celestial earth. The reality of this place communicates itself to human beings through images, for images are perceived through sense-perception but may also carry an immaterial or divine meaning which appeals to the intellect. These planes are not static strata but dynamic intensifications of the energy of the supreme Divine Being which perpetually pours out through creation, and in neoplatonic terms, expresses itself in the middle realm through personifications we call gods, angels or daimons.

In this cosmology, the imagination is not a human faculty projecting fantasies. It is the angelic mode of perception. Angels do not have physical senses, so they perceive through imagination. When humans cultivate the active imagination, they are literally learning to perceive as angels perceive.

The celestial souls are empowered by the desire to return to their particular archangel. Each level of the material cosmos resonates with its soul and thereby with its angel, but the soul or angel of the world is only perceptible through the cultivation of what Corbin terms the active imagination. The imagination is the angelic mode of perception, for being immaterial they do not possess the sense-perception of human beings. Angel, soul and world are not separate entities out there, but modes of perception: the world is perceived through sense, soul through imagination and angel through intellect. As the imagination conveys divine thoughts through images, the very act of imagining becomes a divine act of reconnection.

Voss turns to Corbin's own essay on the mundus imaginalis to describe this interworld as a place of visionary experience. It is the place where all religious and transcendent experience occurs: theophanic visions, dreams, meditative states, artistic inspiration, romantic love. It is a precise order of reality requiring a precise mode of perception.

In his essay 'Mundus Imaginalis,' Corbin gives a detailed exposition of this interworld as a place of visionary revelation and events that are experienced as more vividly real than everyday reality. The active Imagination guides, anticipates, molds sense perception, that is why it transmutes sensory data into symbols. The Burning Bush is only a brushwood fire if it is merely perceived by the sensory organs. In order that Moses may perceive the Burning Bush and hear the Voice calling him, an organ of trans-sensory perception is needed.

The implication is radical: the same object can be perceived as either literal or symbolic depending on the faculty brought to it. The active Imagination couples objective and subjective worlds, literal fact and spiritual meaning. Directed downward, it produces fantasy. Directed toward archetypal realities, it becomes an organ of vision.

Thus the active imagination couples objective and subjective worlds, literal fact and spiritual meaning. When directed towards archetypal realities, it can bring these realities into harmony and resonance with the world, but if it is solely directed downwards towards matter it can only produce images which are fantastic, imaginary, unreal or even absurd. The task of human beings is to purify and liberate the soul so that it may begin to pick up the traces of divine meaning behind the appearances of things.

This brings us to the central figure: the Angel. In Corbin's tradition, the Angel is not a creation of human fantasy but the most real being a person can encounter. Each human soul has a celestial counterpart, a 'Perfect Nature' or Guardian Angel, which is their eternal, perfected individuality.

The Angel is the supreme form of manifestation of Absolute Being in this tradition. Corbin says: 'The Angel is the face that our god takes for us, and each of us finds his god only when he recognises that face.' Such a recognition takes place in the imaginal world. According to this tradition of prophetic philosophy, the active intellect of God can only be encountered through the Angel of Revelation personified as an individual angelic being. It could be speculated upon as an abstract concept, but only fully understood through personal encounter.

The question becomes: how does the earthly ego integrate with its celestial counterpart? This is not an intellectual exercise but a transformation of the whole person. The ego must undergo a painful purification before the encounter can occur. The metaphors are consistent across traditions: travelling to the underworld, fighting the dragon, undergoing the alchemical nigredo.

Each human soul has as its counterpart a celestial soul, who is the eternal and perfected individuality of the person, their transcendent celestial self. The question then becomes how to integrate the earthly ego with this soul and through it with its angel, for it is through such an engagement that the individual becomes fully a Person, an integrated whole, connected to the source of Being yet also active in the world. Making the connection is not easy; it involves breaking through the boundaries of habitual consciousness and opening up to an intensity of existence normally inaccessible.

Voss introduces the term ta'wil, the spiritual hermeneutics that uncovers deeper meanings beyond the literal. This is not an intellectual analysis but a passionate movement of the soul toward the truth it desires. The crucial transition is from allegory to symbol: allegory rephrases what is already known, but symbol discloses something irreducible that cannot be known otherwise.

Ta'wil refers to the process of interiorisation, of restoring the true meaning of a text or image through transmuting the world into symbols. This is the hermeneutical method whereby a sacred text reveals its central, mystical message through the stages of the literal, the allegorical, the symbolic and the anagogic levels of understanding. The crucial point is the transition from allegorical to symbolic interpretation, for it is here that the 'turning upside down' of reality occurs and the spiritual meaning becomes apparent. Corbin says: 'The current attitude is to oppose the real to the imaginary as though to the unreal, to confuse symbol with allegory. Every allegorical interpretation is harmless; the allegory is a disguising of something that is already known. But the appearance of an Image having the quality of a symbol is a primary phenomenon, unconditional and irreducible.'

The power that facilitates this encounter is himma, the force of the heart's desire. Himma is not wishful thinking. It is an intensity of intention so powerful that it can project and realise a being external to oneself. The mystic's himma can create changes in the world by bringing spiritual power to bear on matter.

Corbin defines himma as 'the act of meditating, conceiving, imagining, projecting, ardently desiring. It is the force of an intention so powerful as to project and realise a being external to the being who conceives the intention.' The himma of a mystic can create changes in the world through an intensity of imagination that resonates on the plane of archetypal Ideas; he is thus himself a divine creator who establishes the patterns from which material forms derive. What we call a miracle is the result of such a capacity to bring spiritual power to bear on matter.

Voss closes with an image that ties everything together: the artist as magician. The artist who perceives the Angel through himma and gives it form in art leads the viewer to also perceive it. This is not metaphor. In the imaginal world, to perceive something is to participate in its reality.

To those who have cultivated the direction of affective force through longing and desire, the Angel will reveal itself, often in an intensely private and uncommunicable way, disguised as an event which to an outsider seems no different from any other. Corbin suggests that the artist too may perform the role of magician, creating external forms through his art for the divine quality he has perceived through his himma, and thus leading the viewer or listener also to perceive it.

Voss concludes by placing Corbin in the lineage of Platonic interpreters from Plotinus and Iamblichus through to Jung and Hillman. His importance, she argues, is that he gives us a language for visionary experience at a time when the imagination has been reduced to fantasy. He reconnects the reader with the power of direct spiritual participation, not as historical study but as living reality.

The importance of Corbin's work cannot be overestimated in a world which is blinkered and starved of a sense of the sacred, and which tends to reduce the imagination to fantasy and illusion. He gives us a language in which to speak with penetrating insight about the reality of visionary experience, a place to locate divinatory realisation. He reconnects the reader with what has become popularly known as 'the power of now' through demonstrating that a faithful study of religious experience must involve a move away from the objectifying approach of the historian, towards the position of the mystic for whom it is a living reality.
